title: TPU v1 · Wafer Fabrication core loop: deposition → etch → doping → CMP—the precise grow, pattern, dope, and planarize cycle that builds every transistor in the 256×256 systolic array layer by layer
oneLine: Lithography only draws in resist. The core Wafer Fabrication loop transfers circuitry into silicon and stacks dozens of layers: deposit material by CVD/PVD/ALD, pattern it by lithography, etch away unwanted material or implant ions to set conductivity for transistor source/drain/channel, then planarize with CMP so the next layer can align and focus. Repeating this cycle dozens of times forms the gates, junctions, and interconnects for 256×256 = 65,536 8-bit MACs on a 28 nm, ≤331 mm² die. Any deviation in film thickness, etch depth, implant dose, or planarity can kill the regular large array.

legend.name: ① Deposit
legend.desc: Grow a new blanket layer across the wafer: insulating oxide, conductive metal, or polysilicon gate material. CVD forms films by surface chemical reaction; PVD deposits material physically, often by sputtering; ALD grows one atomic layer at a time for highly precise thickness. Lithography and etch later trim this blanket layer into the required geometry. Every layer of a TPU-class stack begins with deposition.

legend.name: ③ Etch
legend.desc: Use developed resist as a mask and remove exposed material, transferring the resist image into silicon or film. Dry plasma etch is anisotropic and supports vertical sidewalls/fine lines; wet chemical etch is isotropic with useful selectivity. Under-etch leaves incomplete geometry; over-etch damages lower layers. Small depth or sidewall errors become defects across the 256×256 array.

legend.name: ③′ Dope (ion implantation)
legend.desc: Modify selected silicon regions by accelerating boron, phosphorus, arsenic, or related dopant ions into them, setting N-type/P-type conductivity and concentration for transistor source, drain, and channel threshold. Lithography selects location; dose and energy set amount/depth; anneal activates the lattice. Doping changes electrical properties rather than removing material.

legend.name: ④ CMP planarization
legend.desc: Chemical-Mechanical Planarization uses slurry and a polishing pad to flatten topography after a layer. Without a planar surface, later lithography loses focus and linewidth varies. Chips stack dozens of layers, so CMP—especially after interconnect layers—removes steps and returns a flat surface for the next deposition.

legend.name: ⑤ Layer-by-layer loop
legend.desc: Deposition → lithography → etch/doping → CMP completes one layer. Change masks and repeat dozens of times: FEOL first forms active regions and gates, then BEOL stacks metal interconnects. TPU v1's 65,536 MACs, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, and wiring emerge one layer at a time on the 28 nm wafer.

steps.cap: ① Deposit: use CVD/PVD/ALD to blanket-grow this layer's oxide, metal, or polysilicon, providing material to pattern.
② Lithography + ③ etch: draw the layer in resist, then remove uncovered material by dry/wet etch. Lithography draws; etch transfers.
③′ Dope: implant ions into selected silicon, alter conductivity for transistor source/drain/channel, then anneal to activate it.
④ CMP: chemically and mechanically planarize layer topography so the next lithography pass remains in focus.
⑤ Return for the next layer: change masks and repeat. Dozens of cycles form 256×256 = 65,536 MACs, the Unified Buffer, and interconnects on a ≤331 mm² 28 nm die.

before: Identify the layer and process type: determine whether this is dielectric, metal interconnect, gate, or implant, then choose CVD/PVD/ALD and etch versus ion implantation. The wrong process invalidates the layer.

Prepare the layer mask and recipe: verify the OPC mask revision (see 3.2), then set deposition thickness, etch chemistry/time/depth, implant dose/energy, and CMP removal. A 28 nm large chip has tight per-layer windows.
Confirm the lower layer is planar and stack-ready: verify prior CMP and preserve alignment references. Residual topography defocuses current lithography.
Clean and calibrate equipment/chambers: qualify temperature, plasma, slurry, and contamination status for deposition, etch, implant, and CMP. Particles or drift become die-killing defects.
during: Four operations form a layer: grow → pattern → etch/dope → planarize: deposit material → lithographically pattern resist → etch material or implant dopants → CMP the surface. One pass completes one layer and prepares the next; precise coordination across all operations builds the circuit.

Deposition blankets the wafer: CVD reacts gases at the surface, PVD sputters metal, and ALD grows atomic layers for maximum thickness control. Thickness and uniformity are critical because downstream etch and electrical properties inherit any variation.

Etch removes by pattern; depth and sidewall are critical: dry plasma supports vertical fine features, while wet chemistry offers selectivity. Under-etch fails to clear; over-etch attacks lower layers. Small sidewall/depth errors become regular-array defects.

Doping modifies rather than removes material: implant boron/phosphorus/arsenic into selected regions to form source, drain, and channel threshold. Pattern defines location; dose/energy define profile; annealing activates dopants. This makes silicon electrically switchable.
CMP planarization enables vertical stacking: polish after layers, especially interconnect, to remove steps globally. Without flat lower layers, upper lithography loses focus and linewidth control.

Scope note: CVD/PVD/ALD, dry/wet etch, ion implant + anneal, and CMP are standard 28 nm large-chip/PCIe-accelerator processes. TPU v1's private recipes, thicknesses, etch/implant parameters, layer count, and foundry are not public. Its 256×256 array, 24 MiB UB, ≤331 mm² die, and 28 nm node provide the manufactured context.

result.label: Cycle output
result.value: One completed and planarized device or interconnect layer on the wafer

result.label: Four core operations
result.value: Deposition (CVD/PVD/ALD) → lithography → dry/wet etch or ion implantation → CMP

result.label: Deposit vs. etch vs. dope
result.value: Deposit adds material; etch removes material; doping changes conductivity; CMP planarizes

result.label: Transistor formation
result.value: Deposition + etch form gate/source/drain/channel geometry; ion implantation sets conductivity

result.label: Layer count and target
result.value: Dozens of cycles build 256×256 = 65,536 MACs, 24 MiB UB, and multilayer interconnect

result.label: Next handoff
result.value: Planarized layer returns to deposition for the next layer; completed stack proceeds to backside thinning, dicing, and package/test

after: Inspect and measure the completed layer: verify film thickness, etch depth/sidewalls, critical dimensions, implant dose, and surface planarity before burying a defect under later layers.
Advance only after the layer passes: after CMP, confirm planarity and alignment references, then change masks and repeat. An error in one layer propagates upward.

Feed measurements back into the process window: adjust deposition time, etch dose, implant energy, and CMP removal to improve within-wafer and wafer-to-wafer uniformity. Persistent drift triggers equipment or upstream-layout review.

Complete all layers and hand off downstream: after dozens of FEOL transistor and BEOL interconnect cycles, the 256×256 array and wiring are complete. Proceed to backside thinning, dicing, and package/test in later ⑫⑬ sections.
Preserve traceability: archive per-layer recipes and metrology for yield and failure analysis without publicly disclosing private TPU process figures.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the layer-by-layer core loop of CVD/PVD/ALD deposition, dry/wet etch, ion implantation + anneal, and CMP is standard manufacturing practice, not private TPU v1 disclosure)
